task.goal: prove exploitable APK attack paths from attacker entrypoint to visible impact

knowledge: Android App Vulnerability Hunting — planning discipline. Start by collecting the attack surface before drilling into any single path. Attack-surface collection priority: exported components (Activity/Service/Receiver/Provider), deep links, AIDL, dynamic receivers, WebView bridges, PendingIntent flows, broadcasts, cross-app channels. Then decompose along a composite exploit chain — pick the smallest chain that could prove source -> controlled object -> sink -> guard failure -> impact. Each intent is one link in such a chain.

Composite exploit-chain matrix (use these chain shapes to decide which directions to open; pick the smallest that matches observed code behavior):
- Exported entry -> Bundle/key mismatch -> intent redirect -> private component (validates one extra/key but launches or returns a different caller-controlled Intent/component/selector/flags)
- Exported entry -> Parcelable/Serializable parsing -> auth/role confusion -> protected sink (caller-controlled object field becomes role/package/account/command/file path/URI before authorization)
- Provider traversal/data leak -> URI grant/result leak -> private file disclosure (Provider path/query/file handle attacker-controlled, exposed via grant/setResult/FileProvider)
- Provider call/batch/oracle -> guard bypass -> protected rows/action (call/applyBatch/bulkInsert/getType reaches data not protected like CRUD)
- WebView URL/deeplink -> JS bridge -> native sink (deep link/HTML controls WebView bridge reaching component/provider/file/token/command sink)
- WebView intent scheme -> Intent redirect -> grant/private component (intent:// or Intent.parseUri launched under app identity without stripping)
- Mutable PendingIntent/fill-in -> victim identity action -> URI grant/private component (mutable/fill-in/replayable PendingIntent dispatched as victim app)
- Ordered broadcast/service command -> protected action -> result/reply leak (external action/extras/Messenger/AIDL/onStartCommand reaches sensitive work, leaks via result/replyTo/notification)
- Task/fragment/UI pivot -> credential/approval action -> persisted state leak (external navigation chooses fragment/task/dialog/lifecycle, moves credential/approval/sensitive state)

Confidence bands: proven 1.0 (decompiled source/manifest/trace output that can be re-read), inferred 0.5-0.7 (cross-process/cross-version/dynamic-dispatch resolved by assumption), speculative 0.2-0.4 (observed pattern shape only, unconfirmed reachability). Promotion needs chain confidence >= 0.7 (weakest link). When the evaluator rejects a fact, fail its intent (with the rejection reason) rather than re-creating near-duplicate work.
prompt.instructions: Collect the attack surface first, then decompose along composite chains into bounded intents. Fail intents whose evidence the evaluator rejects.

knowledge: Probe-first investigation strategy. Do NOT start by reading source broadly. Probe the attack surface with cheap high-signal queries first, then follow the specific trace your intent asks about: (1) manifest and component metadata — exported components, intent filters/deep links, AIDL, dynamic receivers, provider authorities; (2) follow the controlled value through helpers, IPC, component/WebView/provider boundaries to the sink or a blocker, citing the exact call chain; (3) load only the references your trace crosses — do not broaden into sibling surfaces.

Evidence you must cite in every fact: the manifest line or component declaration; the source location (class/method/line) where the controlled value is read; the call chain from entry to sink; the guard result (present, absent, or bypassed); the visible impact. If static tracing stops short, say so explicitly rather than overstating.

Evidence kinds — tag each fact's role in a chain by stating the kind(s) at the start of the description (e.g. 'entrypoint:', 'control+sink+guard:'): entrypoint (component type, exported/trigger condition, trigger syntax), reachability (attacker action reaches the path), control (attacker-controlled value reaches sink argument), guard (guard passes/bypassed/absent), sink (dangerous operation), impact (visible consequence). A proven finding needs all six along a chain.

High-signal single-pattern routing: intent redirect (forwarded Intent/Uri/ClipData/selector/component/package/flags; package equality alone is not enough because component has precedence); object parsing (Bundle key mismatch, Parcelable/Serializable crossing trust boundary, unsafe class loader, field used as authority); provider leak (exported/grant-reachable provider returns protected data, builds SQL, proxies attacker URI); URI grant/result leak (setResult, grantUriPermission, persistable grants, broad FileProvider roots); WebView (URL validation bypass, bridge exposure, file/content access, intent-scheme launch); PendingIntent (mutable/fill-in/replayable/stored dispatched as victim); broadcast/service (dynamic receiver, ordered broadcast, weak custom permission, Messenger, AIDL/bound service, notification leak); Bundle/Parcel mismatch (object checked by guard differs from object later executed after unparcel/reserialize/nested-Intent extraction).
prompt.instructions: Probe-first: inspect manifest, exported components, deep links before reading source. Cite concrete evidence (manifest line, source location) in every fact.

knowledge: Exploitability gate — accept a fact as evidence only if it contributes to a chain that can satisfy ALL FOUR conditions: (1) Reachable: the attacker can trigger the path; (2) Controllable: the attacker controls the security-relevant field or object; (3) Deeply traced: the value is followed through helpers, callbacks, IPC/component/WebView/provider/Binder boundaries to the sink or a blocker; (4) Impactful: the path causes a visible security consequence. A proven finding needs the full six-kind chain: entrypoint -> reachability -> control -> guard -> sink -> impact.

Severity (use when accepting an impact-class fact): CRITICAL (remote/low-friction system/root/trusted-domain code execution, persistent device compromise, silent install, device takeover); HIGH (high-value data disclosure, app-sandbox arbitrary read, credential/token theft, code execution in app process, meaningful security-interaction bypass); MEDIUM (real bounded impact requiring local app, interaction, or device/version condition); LOW (fragile/limited UI/social/recon impact); IGNORED (unreachable, non-security, crash-only, no visible impact). Raise when chaining is clean or no malicious install is needed; lower when physical access, narrow scope, or unstable exploitation apply.

Rejection rules — reject when ANY hold: evidence is inline-only/speculative with no manifest or source trace; the exact sink path is rebuilt from trusted constants; dangerous fields are stripped before the sink; a non-bypassable guard covers the exact method; write-only state with no readback or impact; the path is normalized and confined; impact is only crash/compatibility with no security consequence; component reachability with no Impactful outcome; the object checked by a guard differs from the object later executed.
prompt.instructions: Apply the four-element gate (reachable/controllable/deeply-traced/impactful) and reject facts with inline-only evidence.
Hint when the hunt drifts (repeated dead-ends, duplicate routes, ignored rejection verdicts, stalled intents, or missing evidence-gate review). Surface blind spots in the attack-chain coverage.
